data.text: I’m reading the bead contract and the surrounding DDx/Fizeau code paths first so I can decide whether this is a clean implementation or needs decomposition. After that I’ll either make the scoped changes and verify them, or write the required no-changes rationale if the bead is too large for a single pass.

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:63:{"id":"corpus-42801b4c2e9b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"agent: hardcoded 15-minute DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout kills thinking-route-target requests; not configurable","description":"Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30
  const DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute

This is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every upstream-service regardless of route-target class. It is set on every service path:
  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  Upstream-serviceTimeout: DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout
  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           Upstream-serviceTimeout: DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout

The comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'

But that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:
  const DefaultUpstream-serviceIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute

The 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.

Concrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):

1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)
   - status: no_changes
   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)
   - log: 'WARN upstream-service error, retrying attempt=1 err="upstream-service request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s" delay=1s'
   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.

2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)
   - All 3 hit 'upstream-service request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt
   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each

Why thinking route-targets hit this:
qwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning route-targets can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the runner considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the runner is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.

Why arbitrary timeouts are not helping:

a) Same value for cloud upstream-services (claude, codex) and local upstream-services (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking route-targets routinely need >15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.

b) No way to opt out of the cap per upstream-service, per route-target, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.

c) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.

d) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking route-target the timeout doesn't mean 'broken upstream-service', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.","acceptance":"AC1. Upstream-serviceTimeout is configurable per-project via .ddx/config.yaml under agent.routing or agent.endpoints — e.g. agent.endpoints[upstream-service].request_timeout_seconds — and the current 15-minute hardcoded constant becomes a default that can be overridden upward (or removed entirely) per upstream-service.

AC2. Upstream-serviceTimeout is configurable per-tier via the route-target-overrides path: cheap tier on a known thinking route-target (qwen3.6, deepseek-r1, etc.) defaults to a longer cap (e.g. 60 min or unlimited). Cloud chat route-targets keep the 15-min default.

AC3. The idle-read timeout (DefaultUpstream-serviceIdleReadTimeout = 5 min) is the primary stalled-TCP-socket defense; documentation in cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go and the user-facing config docs explain the relationship between idle-read and wall-clock-request timeouts and when each fires.

AC4. When Upstream-serviceTimeout fires, the error message names the exact timeout setting and the path to override it (e.g. 'request_timeout=15m exceeded; configure agent.endpoints.<name>.request_timeout_seconds in .ddx/config.yaml').

AC5. execute-loop / execute-bead --request-timeout DURATION CLI flag exists for one-off overrides during debugging.

AC6. Default for known-thinking-route-target surfaces (upstream-service.type=lmstudio AND route-target name matches qwen3.6/qwen-r1/deepseek-r1 etc., or some other declared 'thinking' attribute) is bumped to a sensible value (e.g. 60 min) without requiring per-project config.","labels":["area:agent","area:routing","area:runner","kind:design-flaw"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-2c63bb95","title":"agent: hardcoded 15-minute DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout kills thinking-route-target requests; not configurable","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:115:{"id":"corpus-7a93d490ad8e","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"bug: embedded agent stalls in post-commit compaction loop","description":"The embedded 'agent' runner can complete a task successfully (commit produced, tests pass) but then fail to terminate, getting stuck in a compaction retry loop with compaction always failing. The parent execute-loop never observes task_succeeded and hangs forever.

## Observed

Running 'ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --runner agent --route-target qwen/qwen3-coder-next' against axon-43ed769b (axon auth-core-types bead):

1. qwen3-coder-next ran the task correctly, producing commit 91d4bdb with 636 LOC of types + 109 green tests + clippy clean. The commit was present in the worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-axon-43ed769b-20260415T005652-ebe7a16a.

2. Instead of terminating, the agent kept generating events. After ~20 minutes the session log was 33 MB (8461+ events, 249 messages) and stuck in:

    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    ...

3. Parent ddx process in __futex_wait. Had to SIGTERM the parent (PID 4141811) and manually fast-forward-merge the worktree commit to land the work.

4. A1 (previous bead) ran the same runner/route-target and terminated correctly in ~14 min with identical-looking commit shape. The difference was bead size: A2's output was a single 633-line Rust file, vs A1's 350-line SQL + 290-line test file. A2 didn't exceed what A1 did — so the context-limit explanation doesn't fully account for it.

## Expected

- After the agent commits and has nothing more to do, it should emit task_succeeded and terminate
- Compaction should either succeed (messages_after < messages_before) or bail out cleanly, not retry indefinitely
- If the context genuinely cannot be compacted, the agent should report a structured failure and preserve the commits, not spin

## Scope

- Detect mid-turn compaction returning success=false repeatedly and bail with a structured 'compaction_stuck' status
- Bound the total number of compaction attempts in a single turn
- Ensure task termination happens immediately after a successful commit + no pending tool calls
- Add a watchdog at the execute-loop level: kill the child if no commit+progress event is observed within a budget

## Evidence

- Session log: /Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
- Rescued commit: 91d4bdb on axon master
- Parent process: PID 4141811 (killed)

## Acceptance

- [ ] Compaction retry loop is bounded and fails structurally
- [ ] Post-commit termination path is deterministic
- [ ] Watchdog detects and terminates stuck sessions with a preserved-commit outcome
- [ ] Regression test reproduces the stuck state (seeded session with large no-op tool calls)","labels":["helix","area:agent","area:exec","kind:bug","gap:stability"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-25a41f51","title":"bug: embedded agent stalls in post-commit compaction loop","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"The embedded 'agent' runner can complete a task successfully (commit produced, tests pass) but then fail to terminate, getting stuck in a compaction retry loop with compaction always failing. The parent execute-loop never observes task_succeeded and hangs forever.

## Observed

Running 'ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --runner agent --route-target qwen/qwen3-coder-next' against axon-43ed769b (axon auth-core-types bead):

1. qwen3-coder-next ran the task correctly, producing commit 91d4bdb with 636 LOC of types + 109 green tests + clippy clean. The commit was present in the worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-axon-43ed769b-20260415T005652-ebe7a16a.

2. Instead of terminating, the agent kept generating events. After ~20 minutes the session log was 33 MB (8461+ events, 249 messages) and stuck in:

    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    ...

3. Parent ddx process in __futex_wait. Had to SIGTERM the parent (PID 4141811) and manually fast-forward-merge the worktree commit to land the work.

4. A1 (previous bead) ran the same runner/route-target and terminated correctly in ~14 min with identical-looking commit shape. The difference was bead size: A2's output was a single 633-line Rust file, vs A1's 350-line SQL + 290-line test file. A2 didn't exceed what A1 did — so the context-limit explanation doesn't fully account for it.

## Expected

- After the agent commits and has nothing more to do, it should emit task_succeeded and terminate
- Compaction should either succeed (messages_after < messages_before) or bail out cleanly, not retry indefinitely
- If the context genuinely cannot be compacted, the agent should report a structured failure and preserve the commits, not spin

## Scope

- Detect mid-turn compaction returning success=false repeatedly and bail with a structured 'compaction_stuck' status
- Bound the total number of compaction attempts in a single turn
- Ensure task termination happens immediately after a successful commit + no pending tool calls
- Add a watchdog at the execute-loop level: kill the child if no commit+progress event is observed within a budget

## Evidence

- Session log: /Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
- Rescued commit: 91d4bdb on axon master
- Parent process: PID 4141811 (killed)

- [ ] Watchdog detects and terminates stuck sessions with a preserved-commit outcome

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:139:{"id":"corpus-9725c274b8e3","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound","description":"The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Downstream consumer DDx ran into this mid-session on a production worker: ~8461 stuck-compaction events accumulated before the hosting process had to be killed.

## Evidence

DDx-side session log:
/Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
Parent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.

## DDx-side workaround (defense in depth, does not fix the root cause)

Commit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 in ddx repo (bead ddx-25a41f51) wraps the embedded agent call with two guards:
1. compaction.start and compaction.end(no_compaction=true) events no longer pulse the caller's inactivity timer — only real-progress events (LLM calls, tool results, commits) extend the timeout.
2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).

The workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.

## Requested upstream fix

- Compaction driver detects its own stuck state via the same heuristic (consecutive success=false/no_compaction=true events) and returns a structured error to the caller
- Eliminates the need for the caller to interpret compaction events as activity signals
- Exposes a clean API signal so consumers can decide whether to retry, escalate, or abort
- Regression test seeds the stuck state and verifies the driver bails instead of looping

## Scope

- Bounded attempt counter inside the compaction loop (not just the caller)
- Structured error: compaction.ErrStuck or similar
- Integration test using a deterministic seed that reproduces the 33MB jsonl pattern

## Relationship to DDx

When this lands upstream, DDx can remove its circuit-breaker workaround and rely on the native error. Until then, DDx keeps both layers as belt-and-suspenders.","acceptance":"(1) Compaction loop bounded with a structural stop. (2) Native structured error signals stuck state to callers. (3) Regression test reproduces the stuck state via seeded read-only tool calls. (4) DDx repo can remove the caller-side circuit breaker once this upstream fix is consumed.","labels":["agent","kind:bug","area:compaction","severity:high","upstream-request"],"bead":{"id":"agent-e5228424","title":"bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Parent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.

2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).

The workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:196:{"id":"corpus-d6e0eda51fa9","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Project home page is orphaned: add it to the sidebar and make the DDx logo a link","description":"## Observed

The project home page (`/nodes/.../projects/<id>/`) renders a useful overview — queue summary (ready/blocked/in-progress) plus three action cards (drain queue, re-align specs, run checks). User feedback: "I see it when I navigate to a new project, then it disappears."

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/NavShell.svelte:26-36` — the sidebar's `pages` array contains: beads, documents, graph, workers, sessions, personas, plugins, commits, efficacy. **There is no entry for the project home/overview.** Once a user clicks any sidebar link, there is no nav-level path back to the home page — only clicking the project in the `ProjectPicker` again, or editing the URL.

Adjacent: the `<span>DDx</span>` brand in the header (`NavShell.svelte:61`) is not a link — common affordance for "go home" is missing.

## Proposed direction

1. Prepend a "Overview" (or "Home") entry to `pages` in `NavShell.svelte`, routing to `/nodes/<nodeId>/projects/<projectId>/` (empty path segment after the project id). Use the `LayoutDashboard` icon that's already imported, or pick a dedicated home icon — design call.
2. Wrap the `DDx` brand in an `<a>` that routes to `/` (node selector) or to the current project's home if one is selected. Pick one and stay consistent.

Trivial change; the biggest decision is whether the brand link goes to node-root or project-home. Recommend project-home when a project is selected (less jarring) and node-root otherwise.","acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer on any project sub-page, I can always return to the project overview with a single click — either from a sidebar entry or from the DDx brand in the header.

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:215:{"id":"corpus-e692e0e87746","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed focused bead with small acceptance surface","title":"feat: compaction user-message tail re-inclusion after compact","description":"<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 — OTel GenAI spans and JSONL session log are separate observability surfaces</adrs>
<governing>SD-006 §Summary Injection — the compaction summary replaces all compacted messages. SD-006 §Codex research — keeps up to 20K tokens of recent user messages alongside summary. FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-06 — compaction no-fit paths must fail closed.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently compaction/compaction.go replaces compacted history with only the summary message. Codex retains the most recent real user messages (up to a token budget) alongside the summary so the route-target retains actual request history.

What to change in compaction/compaction.go (or the Compactor function):
- After generating the summary, collect real user-role messages from the tail of the pre-compaction history (exclude system prefix messages, previous compaction summaries, and tool result messages — only real user messages).
- Accumulate messages from newest to oldest until a configurable token budget is exhausted (default: 20000 tokens, chars/4 heuristic). Per SD-006 §Overlong individual user messages, truncate individual messages that exceed the budget rather than dropping them.
- The replacement history becomes: [summary_message, ...tail_user_messages] ordered so the summary is first and user messages follow in chronological order.
- Expose the budget as a new field UserMessageTailTokens int in CompactionConfig (default 20000; 0 = disabled).
- All existing compaction tests must still pass.
- Add test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion verifying tail messages appear in replacement history.

Governing specs: SD-006, FEAT-001.","acceptance":"go test ./... passes; CompactionConfig gains UserMessageTailTokens field (default 20000); new test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion exists and verifies that after compaction the replacement history contains both the summary message and the most recent real user messages up to the token budget, in chronological order","labels":["helix","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:lib"],"bead":{"id":"agent-d1e7b4a8","title":"feat: compaction user-message tail re-inclusion after compact","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"<context-digest>

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:232:{"id":"corpus-f897af634136","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"runner-test: build golden-master integration suite for real runneres","description":"Build the integration-level evidence suite for the runner migration. The suite must prove the service can drive each supported live runner and can replay recorded interactions deterministically. This is the answer to whether wrapping Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Pi, and opencode is actually supported and tested.

Required runneres for this bead: claude, codex, pi, opencode. Gemini may be included if the service dispatch bead wires it and the capability matrix marks it supported.

Required behaviors to test per runner when supported by its capability matrix:
- run a prompt through Service.Execute
- list or explicitly report route-target support/no-discovery semantics
- pass route-target, reasoning level, permissions, workdir/context, timeout, and metadata
- capture progress events and final normalized text
- capture token usage when the runner exposes it
- capture quota/status data for claude and codex when the capability matrix marks quota supported
- replay the same interaction sequence identically from cassette without credentials

Record mode must be opt-in and must fail fast when credentials, subscription, binary, configured route-target, transport dependency, or quota permissions are missing. Replay mode must be runnable in normal CI without live credentials. Ad-hoc fake binaries are acceptable only for shared parser/process infrastructure; they must not be counted as live runner integration evidence.","acceptance":"1. Integration tests are organized around the public capability matrix: every supported required capability is exercised for every required runner, and unsupported optional capabilities are asserted as unavailable.
2. Record mode can run against live claude, codex, pi, and opencode, fails fast with clear diagnostics when authentication or binaries are missing, and records cassettes with scrubbed environment/credential data.
3. Replay mode runs deterministically without credentials and verifies event order, progress events, final text, usage where available, quota/status where available, routing metadata, and session log metadata.
4. Tests cover route-target selection, configured reasoning level, workdir/context behavior, timeout/idle handling, and metadata propagation through Service.Execute.
5. The suite distinguishes reusable deterministic runner tests from real live-runner evidence and does not claim ad-hoc fake-binary tests as live integration coverage.
6. Documentation explains how to run record mode and replay mode.
7. go test -tags=integration ./... passes in replay mode without live credentials.
8. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:238:{"id":"corpus-011259e83fe4","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","description":"## Observed

User clicks "Drain queue" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/<id>/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.

Verified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.

```
$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json
{
  "id": "worker-20260422T205846-f23f",
  "kind": "execute-loop",
  "state": "running",
  "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",
  ...
}
```

So the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:

```go
if projectID != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {
    continue
}
```

- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.
- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.

These are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.

The global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `""` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.

Note: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.

## Proposed direction

Two options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.

### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site

```go
expectedPath := ""
if projectID != "" {
    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)
    if !ok {
        return nil // unknown project id → no workers
    }
    expectedPath = proj.Path
}
...
if expectedPath != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {
    continue
}
```

### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record

Add `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).

Recommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.

## Scope note

This is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.

## Out of scope

- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).
- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.","acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.

2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**
   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).
   - Start one worker for each project.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-1>)` returns exactly worker 1.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-2>)` returns exactly worker 2.
   - `workers` (global) returns both.
   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.

3. **Regression test — backward compat.**
   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.

4. **End-to-end validation.**
   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.

Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","bug","graphql"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-05b4cc9d","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"## Observed

User clicks "Drain queue" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/<id>/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.

Verified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.

```
$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json
{
  "id": "worker-20260422T205846-f23f",
  "kind": "execute-loop",
  "state": "running",
  "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",
  ...
}
```

So the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.

## Root cause

`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:

```go
if projectID != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {
    continue
}
```

- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.
- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.

These are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.

The global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `""` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.

Note: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.

## Proposed direction

Two options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.

### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site

```go
expectedPath := ""
if projectID != "" {
    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)
    if !ok {
        return nil // unknown project id → no workers
    }
    expectedPath = proj.Path
}
...
if expectedPath != "" && rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {
    continue
}
```

### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record

Add `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).

Recommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.

## Scope note

This is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.

## Out of scope

- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).
- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.","acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.

2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**
   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).
   - Start one worker for each project.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-1>)` returns exactly worker 1.
   - `workersByProject(projectID: <id-2>)` returns exactly worker 2.
   - `workers` (global) returns both.
   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.

3. **Regression test — backward compat.**
   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.

4. **End-to-end validation.**
   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.
   - Same test run against a second project asserts that project's Workers tab does not show project-1's worker.

5. **No regression to unrelated surfaces.**
   - Global `workers` query still returns all workers.
   - Worker detail page still loads by worker id.
   - Workers live-progress subscription still patches rows in place.

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Note on `ddx-69789664` (Workers lifecycle controls): that bead's acceptance assumed Workers pane worked correctly. Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","bug","graphql"]}}

./website/static/demos/01-install.cast:18:[4.738505, "o", "Usage:
  ddx [command]

Available Commands:
  agent       Invoke AI agents with harness dispatch, quorum, and session logging
  bead        Manage beads (portable work items)
  checkpoint  Create, list, or restore named git checkpoints
  completion  Generate completion script
  config      Configure DDx settings
  doc         Document dependency graph and staleness tracking
  doctor      Check installation health and diagnose issues
  exec        Validate and run DDx execution definitions
  help        Help about any command
  init        Initialize DDx in current project
  install     Install a package, resource, or the embedded DDx skills
  installed   List installed packages
  jq          Process JSON with jq expressions
  list        List available DDx resources
  log         Show DDX asset history
  metric      Validate and run metric definitions
  metrics     Show process metrics derived from beads and agent sessions
  outdated    List installed packages with available updates
  persona     Manage AI personas for consistent interactions
  prompts     Manage AI prompts
  search      Search available packages
  server      Start the DDx HTTP and MCP server
  skills      Validate bundled skill packages
  status      Show version and status information
  uninstall   Remove an installed package
  update      Update DDx CLI or installed plugins
  upgrade     Upgrade DDx to the latest version
  verify      Verify installed package integrity
  version     Show version information
  work        Work the bead execution queue

Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
  -h, --help                       help for ddx
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output

Use "ddx [command] --help" for more information about a command.
"]

./docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md:54:6. **Context rot is real; bounded context execution is the response.** LLM output quality decays as a single context window fills — well before the hard token limit. Long-running agent sessions accumulate transcript, tool noise, and failed attempts that compete with the original instructions; the agent at hour one is not the agent at minute one. The structural fix is **bounded context execution** — also known as the **Ralph loop**: every unit of work runs in a fresh, narrowly-scoped context against an explicit contract (a bead with acceptance criteria), with persistent state landing on disk as evidence rather than carried forward as transcript. DDx implements this with `ddx agent execute-bead` (single bounded attempt in an isolated worktree) and `ddx work` (queue drain that re-enters the loop with a fresh context per bead). See `website/content/docs/concepts/bounded-context-execution.md` for the full treatment.

./website/content/features/_index.md:54:The execute-loop is the operational shape of **bounded context execution** — sometimes called the **Ralph loop**. LLM output quality decays as a single context window fills: transcript, tool output, and failed attempts accumulate and compete with the original instructions. We call this decay **context rot**, and it is why long-running agent sessions are unreliable even on frontier models.
./website/content/features/_index.md:66:Every execution writes a bundle under `.ddx/executions/<timestamp>-<hash>/` with the prompt, the agent transcript, token and cost telemetry, the diff produced, and the merge outcome. Evidence stays in the repository, attached to the commit, so future reviewers (human or model) can reconstruct what happened without rerunning the agent.

The ten domain principles below are the load-bearing claims behind that factory. Each one is cited in real design and review decisions; each one has a deep page that walks through what it means and how DDx applies it.

  <bead id="axon-dccbdf73">
    <title>auth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension</title>
    <description>
Implement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server.

## What you're building

An axum middleware layer that runs on every data-plane request. It validates the JWT, installs a (user_id, tenant_id, grants) tuple into the request extension, and returns a typed AuthError on any failure. Downstream handlers read the extension and trust it completely.

## Scope — exactly what to add

### New file: `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`

A module exposing two things:

1. A `JwtIssuer` helper that signs a `JwtClaims` into a compact JWT string. HS256 is the only algorithm required for this bead. The signing key is a `Vec<u8>` passed in at construction. Provide `issue(&self, claims: &JwtClaims) -> Result<String, AuthError>` and a `verify(&self, token: &str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError>` for round-tripping in tests.

2. An axum middleware `async fn jwt_verify_layer` that runs the 8-step verification order below. On success, installs a `ResolvedIdentity { user_id, tenant_id, grants }` into the request extension. On failure, returns a response built via `AuthError::into_response()` so the mapping to HTTP status + error.code is consistent.

The middleware also owns an `InMemoryRevocationCache` (a small `tokio::sync::RwLock<HashSet<Uuid>>` is fine; nothing fancy) and falls back to SQL (via the storage adapter's new `is_jti_revoked` method) on cache miss.

### Extend: `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs`

- Add `ResolvedIdentity { pub user_id: UserId, pub tenant_id: TenantId, pub grants: Grants }`.
- Add `impl AuthError` with `pub fn status_code(&self) -> u16` returning 401 or 403 per ADR-018 §4 failure table, and `pub fn error_code(&self) -> &'static str` returning the stable code string. (An axum `IntoResponse` impl can live in axon-server.)
- Add `impl Grants` with `pub fn find_database(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&GrantedDatabase>` and `impl GrantedDatabase` with `pub fn has_op(&self, op: Op) -> bool`.

### Extend: `crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs` (trait)

Add one method to the `StorageAdapter` trait:

```rust
async fn is_jti_revoked(&self, jti: Uuid) -> Result<bool, AxonError>;
```

Implement it in `crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs` (store a `HashSet<Uuid>`; default-empty), `crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs`, and `crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs` (`SELECT 1 FROM credential_revocations WHERE jti = $1`). The A1 schema (`auth_schema.rs`) already has the `credential_revocations` table — just query it.

### Wire into: `crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs`

Re-export `auth_pipeline::{jwt_verify_layer, JwtIssuer, InMemoryRevocationCache}`. Don't wire it into the main router yet — that's D-phase work. For this bead, the middleware just needs to exist and be testable in isolation.

## Verification order (normative — ADR-018 §4)

1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` from the request. Missing header → `AuthError::Unauthenticated` (401). The `--no-auth` bypass is NOT part of this bead — that's E2.
2. Parse and verify the JWT signature with HS256 against the issuer's key. Signature invalid → `CredentialInvalid` (401). Malformed structure → `CredentialMalformed` (401). `aud` as a JSON array → `CredentialMalformed` (this falls out of the A2 `parse_claims` wrapper — use it).
3. Check `nbf` and `exp` against `SystemTime::now()` with a 30s skew budget. `exp` more than 30s in the past → `CredentialExpired` (401). `nbf` more than 30s in the future → `CredentialNotYetValid` (401).
4. Check `claims.jti` against the revocation cache, then SQL on miss. Hit → `CredentialRevoked` (401). Populate the cache on SQL hit to speed up subsequent checks.
5. Compare `claims.aud` to the `{tenant}` segment extracted from the URL path. Mismatch → `CredentialWrongTenant` (**403**, not 401 — see the table). The URL extraction path is: look at `request.uri().path()` and parse the leading `/tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/...` segments. For this bead, implement a small helper `fn extract_tenant_database(path: &str) -> Option<(&str, &str)>` that returns `None` for non-data-plane paths so they skip the middleware.
6. Look up the `sub` UUID in the `users` table via a new `StorageAdapter::get_user(UserId) -> Result<Option<User>, AxonError>` method. User not found or `user.suspended_at_ms.is_some()` → `UserSuspended` (401).
7. Check tenant membership: a new `StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member(TenantId, UserId) -> Result<Option<TenantMember>, AxonError>`. Missing → `NotATenantMember` (403).
8. Walk `claims.grants.databases[]` looking for an entry matching the URL `{database}`. None → `DatabaseNotGranted` (403). Found → compute the required op from the HTTP method (GET = Read, POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE = Write) and intersect with the grant's ops. Empty intersection → `OpNotGranted` (403). Admin-requiring routes are out of scope for this bead — the middleware only handles Read/Write classification.
9. Build `ResolvedIdentity` and install it via `request.extensions_mut().insert(resolved)`.

## Error mapping — must exactly match ADR-018 §4

The mapping table is copied here verbatim. Do not invent variants; the 14 AuthError variants already exist in axon-core.

| Failure | Status | `error.code` |
|---|---|---|
| No Authorization header | 401 | `unauthenticated` |
| Header not `Bearer <token>` or base64 broken | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| `aud` as JSON array | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| Signature invalid | 401 | `credential_invalid` |
| `exp` in past (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_expired` |
| `nbf` in future (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_not_yet_valid` |
| `jti` revoked | 401 | `credential_revoked` |
| `iss` unknown | 401 | `credential_foreign_issuer` — for this bead, reject if `claims.iss != issuer.issuer_id` |
| `aud` ≠ URL tenant | **403** | `credential_wrong_tenant` |
| `sub` suspended/deleted | 401 | `user_suspended` |
| `sub` not a tenant member | **403** | `not_a_tenant_member` |
| URL database not in grants | **403** | `database_not_granted` |
| Op not in grant | **403** | `op_not_granted` |

Note the 401 vs 403 split: credential-level problems are 401, scope-level problems are 403.

## Testing — all must pass for this bead to be complete

### Unit tests in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs`

For every row of the error mapping table, build the minimal defective request and assert the middleware produces exactly that `(status, error_code)` pair. Table-driven with `#[test]` or a single `#[test]` that iterates an array of cases.

Additionally:

- `jwt_roundtrip_succeeds`: issue a valid JWT with `JwtIssuer::issue`, verify it with `JwtIssuer::verify`, assert claims match.
- `valid_jwt_populates_request_extension`: run the full middleware on a valid JWT, assert the response is 200 and the handler sees `ResolvedIdentity` in extensions.
- `revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second`: issue a valid JWT, verify it succeeds, insert the jti into the revocation cache, verify the next request returns 401 `credential_revoked`. Do NOT use sleep — invalidate the cache directly.
- `clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected`: construct a JWT with `exp` at `now - 29s` (should succeed) and one with `exp` at `now - 31s` (should fail with `credential_expired`). Do the same for `nbf`.
- `ops_matrix`: for each HTTP method in {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE} and each op set in the grant, assert the expected 200/403 outcome.

### Integration test in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs`

Build a minimal axum router with one dummy route `GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping`. Install the middleware. Hit it with:
- No auth header → 401 `unauthenticated`
- Valid JWT with `grants: [{name: "orders", ops: [read]}]` → 200
- Valid JWT with the wrong tenant in aud → 403 `credential_wrong_tenant`
- Valid JWT with grants for a different database → 403 `database_not_granted`

### axon-sim workload `inv_018_grant_enforcement`

**If this is too large for one pass, skip the axon-sim workload and note it in the commit message for a follow-up bead.** The core verification must ship; the simulation workload can follow. Do not block the whole bead on the workload.

## Crate dependencies you will need

Add to `crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml` under `[dependencies]`:
```toml
jsonwebtoken = "9"
```

Everything else (axum, tower, tokio, uuid, serde_json, http) is already in the workspace.

## Context you must know about

### A1 landed: schema
- `crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs` has the SQL for `users`, `user_identities`, `tenant_users`, `credential_revocations`, etc.
- `user_identities.external_id` (NOT `subject`) — already corrected.
- Migration runs via `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` / `_postgres`.

### A2 landed: types
- `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` has all the types: `TenantId`, `UserId`, `User`, `UserIdentity`, `Tenant`, `TenantMember`, `TenantRole`, `Op`, `GrantedDatabase`, `Grants`, `JwtClaims`, `AuthError` (14 variants).
- `parse_claims(&str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError>` wraps serde_json and maps errors to `CredentialMalformed`. USE IT.
- `AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14` is enforced via compile-time exhaustive match.
- Delegation helper on `TenantRole`: `admin.can_delegate(op)` / etc.

### Files you should NOT touch
- Anything outside `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`, `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` (additive), `crates/axon-storage/src/{adapter,memory,sqlite,postgres}.rs` (additive), and the two new test files.
- The existing `crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs` — that's the Tailscale whois path. Leave it alone. Your pipeline is a new module.

## Quality bar

You are the implementation step. A post-merge review with opus will verify the result against the AC. If your commit misses an AC item, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher tier. **Don't commit partial work hoping the review slides.** If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write a `no_changes_rationale.txt` explaining exactly what's done and what's blocking, and do NOT commit — the bead will be re-queued.

Specifically: if you cannot make `cargo test -p axon-server` and `cargo clippy -p axon-server -- -D warnings` green, do not commit. Fix the issues first.

- [ ] get_user and get_tenant_member wired similarly
- [ ] ResolvedIdentity lives in axon-core and is installed into the axum request extension
- [ ] cargo test -p axon-server green
- [ ] cargo test -p axon-storage green (new trait methods added)
- [ ] cargo test -p axon-core green (new helpers added)
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

  <instructions>
You are a coding agent implementing a single bead inside an isolated git worktree. Work from the bead description above — it is complete and unambiguous. The AC list is the completion contract.

## How to work

1. **Read first, act second.** Read the governing ADR-018 sections and the existing crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs so you understand the AuthError variants and the parse_claims wrapper. Then read crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs to confirm the credential_revocations table shape. Then start coding.

2. **Cross-reference your work against the AC as you go.** Every AC checkbox must be provably satisfied by a piece of code you can point to. Before committing, re-read the AC and physically tick each one.

3. **Run the verification commands locally before committing.** cargo test and cargo clippy per the AC. If either fails, fix it. Do not commit red code.

4. **Commit once, when everything works.** Do not commit partial or exploratory changes. The bead is a single atomic unit of work.

5. **If you cannot complete the bead**, write `.ddx/executions/b1-enhanced-manual/no_changes_rationale.txt` with (a) what's done, (b) what's blocking, (c) why a follow-up bead is needed. Do not commit red code to "make progress".

## Constraints

- Work only in the files listed under "Scope" and "Files you should NOT touch" above.
- Do not modify CLAUDE.md or add new documentation.
- Do not run `ddx init`.
- Keep the `.ddx/executions/` directory intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- Commit with `git add <specific files> && git commit -m 'feat(auth-pipeline): ... [axon-dccbdf73]'`. Do not `git add -A` — there may be other WIP in the worktree.

## Done definition

`cargo test -p axon-server -p axon-core -p axon-storage` green + `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean + every AC item ticked + one commit. That's done.

connection.\n15\t///\n16\t/// This is idempotent — running it multiple times on the same database\n17\t/// produces the same result without errors or duplicate rows.\n18\tpub fn apply_auth_migrations_sqlite(conn: &rusqlite::Connection) -> Result<(), String> {\n19\t    // Create tenants table.\n20\t    // UNIQUE(name) enforced by the UNIQUE constraint on the name column.\n21\t    conn.execute_batch(\n22\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenants (\n23\t            id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n24\t            name               TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,\n25\t            display_name       TEXT NOT NULL,\n26\t            created_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL,\n27\t            updated_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL\n28\t        )\",\n29\t    )\n30\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"tenants table: {e}\"))?;\n31\t\n32\t    // Create users table.\n33\t    conn.execute_batch(\n34\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (\n35\t            id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n36\t            display_name       TEXT NOT NULL,\n37\t            email              TEXT,\n38\t            created_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL,\n39\t            suspended_at_ms    INTEGER\n40\t        )\",\n41\t    )\n42\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"users table: {e}\"))?;\n43\t\n44\t    // Create user_identities table.\n45\t    // PRIMARY KEY (provider, external_id) enforces UNIQUE(provider, external_id).\n46\t    conn.execute_batch(\n47\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS user_identities (\n48\t            provider       TEXT NOT NULL,\n49\t            external_id    TEXT NOT NULL,\n50\t            user_id        TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),\n51\t            created_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n52\t            PRIMARY KEY (provider, external_id)\n53\t        )\",\n54\t    )\n55\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"user_identities table: {e}\"))?;\n56\t\n57\t    // Create tenant_users table.\n58\t    // PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, user_id) is the composite PK required by ADR-018.\n59\t    conn.execute_batch(\n60\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_users (\n61\t            tenant_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),\n62\t            user_id      TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),\n63\t            role         TEXT NOT NULL,\n64\t            added_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n65\t            PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, user_id)\n66\t        )\",\n67\t    )\n68\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"tenant_users table: {e}\"))?;\n69\t\n70\t    // Create tenant_databases table.\n71\t    conn.execute_batch(\n72\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_databases (\n73\t            tenant_id      TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),\n74\t            database_name  TEXT NOT NULL,\n75\t            created_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n76\t            PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, database_name)\n77\t        )\",\n78\t    )\n79\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"tenant_databases table: {e}\"))?;\n80\t\n81\t    // Create credential_revocations table.\n82\t    revoked_by     TEXT\n87\t        )\",\n88\t    )\n89\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"credential_revocations table: {e}\"))?;\n90\t\n91\t    Ok(())\n92\t}\n93\t\n94\t/// Apply auth/tenancy migrations to {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01DGpwMtL9EAnaRPBK3GqEBs","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t//! Axon server — HTTP gateway, gRPC service, MCP stdio, and authentication.\n2\t//!\n3\t//! # Authentication\n4\t//!\n5\t//! Identity is resolved once per request by [`auth::AuthContext`] and\n6\t//! injected as a typed extension.  Three modes are supported:\n7\t//!\n8\t//! | Mode | Flag | Actor | Role |\n9\t//! |------|------|-------|------|\n10\t//! | `NoAuth` | `--no-auth` | `\"anonymous\"` | Admin |\n11\t//! | `Tailscale` | *(default)* | node name | from ACL tags |\n12\t//! | `Guest` | `--guest-role <role>` | `\"guest\"` | configured role |\n13\t//!\n14\t//! In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\n15\t//! Unix socket (`/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock` by default) and calls\n16\t//! `/localapi/v0/whois?addr=<peer>` to resolve the connecting node's identity.\n17\t//! Resolved identities are cached by peer IP for the duration of\n18\t//! `--auth-cache-ttl-secs` (default 60 s).\n19\t//!\n20\t//! ACL tag → role mapping:\n21\t//! - `tag:axon-admin` → [`Role::Admin`]\n22\t//! - `tag:axon-write` / `tag:axon-agent` → [`Role::Write`]\n23\t//! - `tag:axon-read` → [`Role::Read`]\n24\t//! - no matching tag → `--tailscale-default-role` (default `read`)\n25\t//!\n26\t//! Connections that are not on the tailnet are rejected with HTTP 401 / gRPC\n27\t//! `UNAUTHENTICATED`.  If the Tailscale daemon is unreachable the server\n28\t//! returns HTTP 503 / gRPC `UNAVAILABLE`.\n29\t//!\n30\t//! # Request flow\n31\t//!\n32\t//! ```text\n33\t//! TCP accept\n34\t//!   └─ authenticate_http_request  (gateway.rs middleware)\n35\t//!        ├─ AuthContext::resolve_peer  →  Identity\n36\t//!        └─ insert Identity into request extensions\n37\t//!             └─ route handler\n38\t//!                  ├─ extract Extension<Identity>\n39\t//!                  ├─ identity.require_read() / require_write() / require_admin()\n40\t//!                  └─ handler logic\n41\t//! ```\n42\t//!\n43\t//! gRPC follows the same pattern via [`service::AxonServiceImpl::authorize`].\n44\tpub mod actor_scope;\n45\tpub mod auth;\n46\tmod collection_listing;\n47\tpub(crate) mod embedded_ui;\n48\tpub mod control_plane;\n49\tpub mod control_plane_routes;\n50\tpub mod cors_config;\n51\tpub mod user_roles;\n52\tpub mod gateway;\n53\tpub mod idempotency;\n54\tpub(crate) mod mcp_http;\n55\tpub mod mcp_stdio;\n56\tpub mod rate_limit;\n57\tpub mod schema_registry;\n58\tpub mod serve;\n59\tpub mod service;\n60\tpub mod tenant_router;\n61\t\n62\tpub use auth::{AuthContext, AuthMode, Identity, Role};\n63\tpub use mcp_stdio::run_mcp_stdio;\n64\tpub use service::AxonServiceImpl;\n65\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01TSMSYvQzdh78YH3Sxyiy8q","session_id":"80f0eb9d-4064-4d06-8560-e247ce1170f2","uuid":"b2e10ba8-f883-42a2-b408-1004661a2ce3","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:30:53.127Z"}

Transactions use SQLite's `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` / `COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK`\n32\t/// statements. `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` acquires a write lock up-front, eliminating\n33\t/// the TOCTOU window that exists when a read and write are issued as separate\n34\t/// statements.\n35\tpub struct SqliteStorageAdapter {\n36\t    conn: Mutex<Connection>,\n37\t    /// `true` while a `BEGIN` has been issued but not yet committed or rolled back.\n38\t    in_tx: bool,\n39\t}\n40\t\n41\timpl SqliteStorageAdapter {\n42\t    /// Opens (or creates) a SQLite database at the given path.\n43\t    pub fn open(path: &str) -> Result<Self, AxonError> {\n44\t        let conn = Connection::open(path).map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\n45\t        let adapter = Self {\n46\t            conn: {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01GZqGkQtHB58eVVTcpTZqa8","type":"tool_result","content":"0\t//! Role-based and attribute-based access control for Axon (FEAT-012).\n1\t//!\n2\t//! # Layers\n3\t//!\n4\t//! **Layer 1 — RBAC** ([`Role`], [`CallerIdentity`], [`Operation`])\n5\t//!\n6\t//! Four built-in roles (`Admin > Write > Read > None`) control access to\n7\t//! Axon operations.  Roles are derived from the identity provider (Tailscale\n8\t//! ACL tags, OIDC claims, etc.) by the server layer and passed into handlers\n9\t//! as a [`CallerIdentity`].  Handlers call [`CallerIdentity::check`] to\n10\t//! enforce the minimum required role.\n11\t//!\n12\t//! **Layer 2 — Field-level masking** ([`MaskPolicy`])\n13\t//!\n14\t//! A `MaskPolicy` hides individual entity fields from callers whose role\n15\t//! falls below a configured minimum.  Applied by [`CallerIdentity::apply_masks`]\n16\t//! before returning entity data to the caller.  Admins always see all fields.\n17\t//!\n18\t//! **Layer 3 — Collection write control** ([`WritePolicy`])\n19\t//!\n20\t//! A `WritePolicy` sets a per-collection minimum write role and marks fields\n21\t//! as immutable after creation.  Checked by the handler before any mutation.\n22\t//!\n23\t//! **Layer 4 — Database-scoped grants** ([`DatabaseGrant`], [`GrantRegistry`])\n24\t//!\n25\t//! Grants scope a role to a specific database (or `\"*\"` for all databases).\n26\t//! Without a matching grant a caller has `Role::None` on that database.\n27\t//! Admins with a global grant bypass per-database checks.  The grant registry\n28\t//! is stored in `__axon_policies__` and enforced at the routing layer.\n29\t\n30\tuse serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};\n31\t\n32\tuse crate::error::AxonError;\n33\t\n34\t/// Built-in roles for Axon RBAC (FEAT-012).\n35\t///\n36\t/// Roles are ordered by privilege: `Admin > Write > Read > None`.\n37\t/// When a user has multiple roles (e.g., from multiple ACL tags),\n38\t/// the highest-privilege role wins.\n39\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]\n40\tpub enum Role {\n41\t    /// No access. Explicitly denied.\n42\t    None = 0,\n43\t    /// Read entities, query, traverse, browse audit log.\n44\t    Read = 1,\n45\t    /// Create, update, delete entities and links in any collection.\n46\t    Write = 2,\n47\t    /// All operations including drop and schema changes.\n48\t    Admin = 3,\n49\t}\n50\t\n51\timpl Role {\n52\t    /// Parse a role from a Tailscale ACL tag or string name.\n53\t    ///\n54\t    /// Accepted inputs (both full `tag:` prefix and bare name):\n55\t    ///\n56\t    /// | Input | Role |\n57\t    /// |-------|------|\n58\t    /// | `\"tag:axon-admin\"` / `\"tag:admin\"` / `\"admin\"` | `Admin` |\n59\t    /// | `\"tag:axon-write\"` / `\"tag:axon-agent\"` / `\"tag:write\"` / `\"write\"` | `Write` |\n60\t    /// | `\"tag:axon-read\"` / `\"tag:read\"` / `\"read\"` | `Read` |\n61\t    /// | anything else | `None` |\n62\t    ///\n63\t    /// `tag:axon-agent` is an alias for `write` — the conventional tag for\n64\t    /// automated agent workloads that need read/write but not admin access.\n65\t    pub fn from_tag(tag: &str) -> Self {\n66\t        match tag {\n67\t            \"tag:axon-admin\" | \"tag:admin\" | \"admin\" => Role::Admin,\n68\t            \"tag:axon-write\" | \"tag:axon-agent\" | \"tag:write\" | \"write\" => Role::Write,\n69\t            \"tag:axon-read\" | \"tag:read\" | \"read\" => Role::Read,\n70\t            _ => Role::None,\n71\t        }\n72\t    }\n73\t\n74\t    /// Determine the highest-privilege role from a set of tags.\n75\t    pub fn from_tags(tags: &[String]) -> Self {\n76\t        tags.iter()\n77\t            .map(|t| Role::from_tag(t))\n78\t            .max()\n79\t            .unwrap_or(Role::None)\n80\t    }\n81\t}\n82\t\n83\timpl std::fmt::Display for Role {\n84\t    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {\n85\t        match self {\n86\t            Role::None => write!(f, \"none\"),\n87\t            Role::Read => write!(f, \"read\"),\n88\t            Role::Write => write!(f, \"write\"),\n89\t            Role::Admin => write!(f, \"admin\"),\n90\t        }\n91\t    }\n92\t}\n93\t\n94\t/// The kind of operation being attempted.\n95\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]\n96\tpub enum Operation {\n97\t    /// Read an entity, query, traverse, audit.\n98\t    Read,\n99\t    /// Create, update, delete entities/links.\n100\t    Write,\n101\t    /// Drop collections, modify schemas, manage namespaces.\n102\t    Admin,\n103\t}\n104\t\n105\timpl Operation {\n106\t    /// The minimum role required for this operation.\n107\t    pub fn required_role(self) -> Role {\n108\t        match self {\n109\t            Operation::Read => Role::Read,\n110\t            Operation::Write => Role::Write,\n111\t            Operation::Admin => Role::Admin,\n112\t        }\n113\t    }\n114\t}\n115\t\n116\t/// A simple field-equality filter used as an agent scope constraint\n117\t/// (FEAT-022 / ADR-016).\n118\t///\n119\t/// When attached to a [`CallerIdentity`], the agent guardrails layer rejects\n120\t/// any mutation whose target entity data does not satisfy\n121\t/// `data[field] == value`. V1 supports only field-equality predicates;\n122\t/// compound and range filters are deferred.\n123\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]\n124\tpub struct EntityFilter {\n125\t    /// The top-level data field to compare.\n126\t    pub field: String,\n127\t    /// The required value for that field.\n128\t    pub value: serde_json::Value,\n129\t}\n130\t\n131\timpl EntityFilter {\n132\t    /// Convenience constructor.\n133\t    pub fn new(field: impl Into<String>, value: serde_json::Value) -> Self {\n134\t        Self {\n135\t            field: field.into(),\n136\t            value,\n137\t        }\n138\t    }\n139\t\n140\t    /// Returns `true` when the JSON object `data` satisfies this filter.\n141\t    ///\n142\t    /// Returns `false` when `data` is not a JSON object, when the field is\n143\t    /// absent, or when the value does not equal [`Self::value`].\n144\t    pub fn matches(&self, data: &serde_json::Value) -> bool {\n145\t        data.get(&self.field).is_some_and(|v| v == &self.value)\n146\t    }\n147\t}\n148\t\n149\t/// Identity context for a request.\n150\t///\n151\t/// Populated by the auth middleware from the identity provider.\n152\t/// In `--no-auth` mode, all requests get `CallerIdentity::anonymous()`.\n153\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]\n154\tpub struct CallerIdentity {\n155\t    /// Actor name for audit log entries.\n156\t    pub actor: String,\n157\t    /// The caller's effective role (highest privilege from all their tags).\n158\t    pub role: Role,\n159\t    /// Optional scope constraint enforced by the agent guardrails layer\n160\t    /// (FEAT-022, ADR-016). When `Some`, this caller may only mutate entities\n161\t    /// whose data matches the filter. `None` means no scope restriction.\n162\t    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = \"Option::is_none\")]\n163\t    pub entity_filter: Option<EntityFilter>,\n164\t}\n165\t\n166\timpl CallerIdentity {\n167\t    /// Create an anonymous identity with admin privileges (`--no-auth` mode).\n168\t    pub fn anonymous() -> Self {\n169\t        Self {\n170\t            actor: \"anonymous\".into(),\n171\t            role: Role::Admin,\n172\t            entity_filter: None,\n173\t        }\n174\t    }\n175\t\n176\t    /// Create an identity from a name and role.\n177\t    pub fn new(actor: impl Into<String>, role: Role) -> Self {\n178\t        Self {\n179\t            actor: actor.into(),\n180\t            role,\n181\t            entity_filter: None,\n182\t        }\n183\t    }\n184\t\n185\t    /// Attach an entity scope filter (builder style, FEAT-022).\n186\t    pub fn with_entity_filter(mut self, filter: EntityFilter) -> Self {\n187\t        self.entity_filter = Some(filter);\n188\t        self\n189\t    }\n190\t\n191\t    /// Check whether this caller is authorized for the given operation.\n192\t    ///\n193\t    /// Returns `Ok(())` if authorized, or `Err(AxonError::InvalidOperation)`\n194\t    /// with a descriptive message if not.\n195\t    pub fn check(&self, op: Operation) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n196\t        let required = op.required_role();\n197\t        if self.role >= required {\n198\t            Ok(())\n199\t        } else {\n200\t            Err(AxonError::InvalidOperation(format!(\n201\t                \"permission denied: role '{}' cannot perform {:?} operations (requires '{}')\",\n202\t                self.role, op, required\n203\t            )))\n204\t        }\n205\t    }\n206\t\n207\t    /// Apply field-level masking to entity data based on mask policies.\n208\t    ///\n209\t    /// Admin users see all fields. For other roles, fields listed in a\n210\t    /// mask policy that requires a higher role than the caller's are\n211\t    /// removed from the data.\n212\t    pub fn apply_masks(&self, data: &mut serde_json::Value, policies: &[MaskPolicy]) {\n213\t        if self.role >= Role::Admin {\n214\t            return; // Admin sees all.\n215\t        }\n216\t        if let Some(obj) = data.as_object_mut() {\n217\t            for policy in policies {\n218\t                if self.role < policy.min_role {\n219\t                    obj.remove(&policy.field);\n220\t                }\n221\t            }\n222\t        }\n223\t    }\n224\t}\n225\t\n226\t/// A field-level mask policy (US-046, FEAT-012).\n227\t///\n228\t/// Specifies that a field should be hidden from callers whose role is\n229\t/// below `min_role`. Admin always sees all fields.\n230\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]\n231\tpub struct MaskPolicy {\n232\t    /// The field name to mask.\n233\t    pub field: String,\n234\t    /// Minimum role required to see this field.\n235\t    pub min_role: Role,\n236\t}\n237\t\n238\t// ── Attribute-based write control (US-047, FEAT-012) ────────────────────────\n239\t\n240\t/// Per-collection write policy for attribute-based access control (ABAC).\n241\t///\n242\t/// Stored in the `__axon_policies__` pseudo-collection. Defines:\n243\t/// - Minimum role required to write to this collection\n244\t/// - Fields that are immutable after creation\n245\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]\n246\tpub struct WritePolicy {\n247\t    /// Collection this policy applies to.\n248\t    pub collection: String,\n249\t    /// Minimum role required for write operations.\n250\t    pub min_write_role: Role,\n251\t    /// Fields that cannot be modified after the initial create.\n252\t    #[serde(default)]\n253\t    pub immutable_fields: Vec<String>,\n254\t}\n255\t\n256\timpl WritePolicy {\n257\t    /// Check if a caller has sufficient role to write to this collection.\n258\t    pub fn check_write(&self, caller: &CallerIdentity) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n259\t        if self.in_tx = false;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn abort_tx(&mut self) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if !self.in_tx {\n            return Ok(());\n        }\n        self.block(self.client.batch_execute(\"ROLLBACK\"))?;\n        self.in_tx = false;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn create_database(&mut self, name: &str) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if self.database_exists(name)? {\n            return Err(AxonError::AlreadyExists(format!(\"database '{name}'\")));\n        }\n\n        self.block(\n            self.client\n                .execute(\"INSERT INTO databases (name) VALUES ($1)\", &[&name]),\n        )?;\n        self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"INSERT INTO namespaces (database_name, name) VALUES ($1, $2)\",\n            &[&name, &DEFAULT_SCHEMA],\n        ))?;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn list_databases(&self) -> Result<Vec<String>, AxonError> {\n        let rows = self.block(\n            self.client\n                .query(\"SELECT name FROM databases ORDER BY name ASC\", &[]),\n        )?;\n        Ok(rows.iter().map(|row| row.get(\"name\")).collect())\n    }\n\n    fn drop_database(&mut self, name: &str) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if !self.database_exists(name)? {\n            return Err(AxonError::NotFound(format!(\"database '{name}'\")));\n        }\n\n        let doomed = self.database_collection_keys(name)?;\n        self.purge_links_for_collections(&doomed)?;\n        self.block(\n            self.client\n                .execute(\"DELETE FROM entities WHERE database_name = $1\", &[&name]),\n        )?;\n        self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"DELETE FROM collection_views WHERE database_name = $1\",\n            &[&name],\n self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"DELETE FROM namespaces\n                 WHERE database_name = $1 AND name = $2\",\n            &[&namespace.database, &namespace.schema],\n        ))?;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn list_namespace_collections(\n        &self,\n        namespace: &Namespace,\n    ) -> Result<Vec<CollectionId>, AxonError> {\n        if !self.namespace_exists(namespace)? {\n            return Err(AxonError::NotFound(format!(\"namespace '{namespace}'\")));\n        }\n\n        let rows = self.block(self.client.query(\n ))?;\n        Ok(rows\n            .iter()\n            .map(|row| {\n                let name: String = row.get(\"name\");\n                CollectionId::new(name)\n            })\n            .collect())\n    }\n\n    fn append_audit_entry(&mut self, mut entry: AuditEntry) -> Result<AuditEntry, AxonError> {\n        if entry.timestamp_ns == 0 {\n            entry.timestamp_ns = SystemTime::now()\n                .duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH)\n                .map(|d| d.as_nanos() as u64)\n                .unwrap_or(0);\n        }\n\n        let entry_json = transaction_id, entry_json)\n                 VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4, $5, $6, $7, $8)\n                 RETURNING id\",\n            &[\n                &(entry.timestamp_ns as i64),\n u64;\n\n        Ok(entry)\n    }\n\n    fn put_schema(&mut self, schema: &CollectionSchema) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        let key = self.resolve_catalog_key(&schema.collection)?;\n        let => Ok(None),\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn get_schema_version(\n        &self,\n        collection: &CollectionId,\n        version: u32,\n    ) -> Result<Option<CollectionSchema>, AxonError> {\n u32,\n                    row.get::<_, i64>(\"created_at_ns\") as u64,\n                )\n            })\n            .collect())\n    }\n\n    fn delete_schema(&mut self, collection: &CollectionId) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        let key = self.resolve_catalog_key(collection)?;\n        self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"DELETE FROM schemas\n                 WHERE collection = $1 AND database_name = $2 AND schema_name = $3\",\n            &[\n                &key.collection.as_str(),\n                &key.namespace.database,\n                &key.namespace.schema,\n self.resolve_catalog_key(&view.collection)?;\n        if !self.collection_exists_in_namespace(&key.collection, &key.namespace)? {\n            return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(format!(\n i32>(\"version\"));\n        let next_version = current_version + 1;\n\n        let updated_at_ns = SystemTime::now()\n            .duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH)\n            .unwrap_or_default()\n versioned.updated_at_ns = Some(updated_at_ns as u64);\n        let view_json = serde_json::to_value(&versioned)?;\n\n        self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"INSERT INTO CONFLICT (database_name, schema_name, collection) DO UPDATE SET\n                     version = EXCLUDED.version,\n                     view_json = EXCLUDED.view_json,\n                     updated_at_ns = EXCLUDED.updated_at_ns,\n                     updated_by = EXCLUDED.updated_by\",\n            &[\n                &key.collection.as_str(),\n                &key.namespace.database,\n                &key.namespace.schema,\n                &next_version,\n                &view_json,\n                &updated_at_ns,\n                &versioned.updated_by,\n            ],\n        ))?;\n        Ok(versioned)\n    }\n\n    fn get_collection_view(\n        &self,\n        collection: &CollectionId,\n    ) -> Result<Option<CollectionView>, AxonError> {\n        let key = self.resolve_catalog_key(collection)?;\n        let rows = self.block(self.client.query(\n            \"SELECT view_json FROM collection_views\n                 WHERE collection = $1 AND database_name = $2 AND schema_name = $3\",\n            &[\n                &key.collection.as_str(),\n                &key.namespace.database,\n                &key.namespace.schema,\n            ],\n still have a pre-fix collection_views table\n        // without the collection -> collections foreign key, and may still\n        // carry metadata rows keyed by the original qualified identifier in\n &key.collection.as_str(),\n                &key.namespace.database,\n                &key.namespace.schema,\n            ],\n        ))?;\n        if raw_collection != key.collection.as_str() {\n            self.block(self.client.execute(\n                \"DELETE FROM schemas\n                     WHERE collection = $1\n                       AND ((database_name = $2 AND schema_name = $3)\n self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"DELETE FROM collections\n                 WHERE name = $1 AND database_name = $2 AND schema_name = $3\",\n            &[\n fn list_collections(&self) -> Result<Vec<CollectionId>, AxonError> {\n        let rows = self.block(self.client.query(\n            \"SELECT name FROM collections\n                 ORDER BY
